clti.label: Figure 1
clti.caption: Prevalence of Peripheral Artery Disease by Age and Sex in Different Income Countries
clti.description: This chart illustrates the prevalence of peripheral artery disease (PAD) in various age groups, segmented by sex and income level of countries. It compares high-income countries (HICs) and low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), showing a trend of increasing prevalence with age.

clti.label: Figure 2
clti.caption: Odds Ratios for Peripheral Artery Disease Risk Factors
clti.description: This chart displays the odds ratios (ORs) for various risk factors associated with peripheral artery disease (PAD) in high-income countries (HICs), low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), and globally. Factors include history of cardiovascular disease, CRP levels, cholesterol levels, smoking status, diabetes, hypertension, BMI, sex, and age.

clti.label: Figure 3
clti.caption: Risk Factors and Atherosclerotic Lesion Levels in Lower Limbs
clti.description: This diagram illustrates the association between various risk factors and the levels of atherosclerotic lesions in the lower limbs. The red overlay indicates the correlation of each risk factor with specific patterns of atherosclerotic disease across different leg segments: iliac, femoro-popliteal, and crural.

clti.label: Figure 4
clti.caption: Flowchart for Investigating Suspected CLTI
clti.description: This flowchart outlines the diagnostic process for patients with suspected chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). It begins with clinical suspicion and guides through physical exams, ABI measurements, and further testing based on results, leading to potential vascular imaging.

clti.label: Figure 5
clti.caption: Imaging Algorithm for CLTI Patients
clti.description: This flowchart outlines the suggested imaging algorithm for patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) who are candidates for revascularization. It includes steps for using duplex ultrasound, CTA, MRA, and diagnostic catheter angiography to achieve adequate imaging of arterial anatomy.

clti.label: Figure 6
clti.caption: Infra-malleolar/Pedal Descriptor in GLASS
clti.description: This figure presents the infra-malleolar/pedal descriptors used in the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) to classify the condition of arteries crossing the ankle into the foot. It includes angiograms illustrating three patterns: P0, P1, and P2.

clti.label: Figure 7
clti.caption: Illustration of Lower Limb Arterial Anatomy and Occlusions
clti.description: This diagram illustrates various anatomical views of the lower limb arteries, highlighting the common femoral artery (CFA), deep femoral artery (DFA), superficial femoral artery (SFA), and popliteal artery (Pop). It shows different patterns of arterial occlusions and stenosis.

clti.label: Figure 8
clti.caption: Infrapopliteal Disease Grading in GLASS System
clti.description: This table illustrates the grading of infrapopliteal disease using the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS). It categorizes disease severity from Grade 0 to Grade 4, based on the extent of stenosis and chronic total occlusion (CTO) in the tibial arteries.

clti.label: Figure 9
clti.caption: GLASS Stage Assignment Table
clti.description: This table outlines the assignment of Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) stages based on femoropopliteal (FP) and infrapopliteal (IP) grades. It provides a matrix to determine GLASS stages I to III using angiographic images.

clti.label: Figure 10
clti.caption: Representative Angiograms of GLASS Stage I Disease Patterns
clti.description: This figure shows angiograms illustrating the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) stage I disease patterns. The target arterial path (TAP) is highlighted in yellow. Two examples (A and B) demonstrate different grades of femoropopliteal (FP) and infrapopliteal (IP) disease.

clti.label: Figure 11
clti.caption: Angiograms of GLASS Stage II Disease Patterns
clti.description: This figure presents representative angiograms illustrating the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) stage II disease patterns. The target arterial path (TAP) is highlighted in yellow, showing the anterior tibial artery and femoropopliteal grade 1 occlusion.

clti.label: Figure 12
clti.caption: Representative Angiograms of GLASS Stage III Patterns
clti.description: This figure displays angiograms illustrating Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) stage III disease patterns. The target arterial path (TAP) is highlighted in yellow, showing different arterial involvements in two cases. Panel A focuses on the peroneal artery, while Panel B highlights the anterior tibial artery.

clti.label: Figure 13
clti.caption: Flowchart for Revascularization in CLTI Patients Using GLASS
clti.description: This flowchart outlines the steps for applying the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) to stage infrainguinal disease in patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) who are candidates for revascularization. It includes obtaining angiographic imaging, defining the target artery path, grading arterial segments, and determining the revascularization strategy.

clti.label: Figure 14
clti.caption: 3D Paradigm for Evidence-Based Revascularization in CLTI
clti.description: This 3D chart illustrates a paradigm for evidence-based revascularization in the treatment of chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). It integrates patient risk, limb severity, and anatomic complexity of disease to guide clinical decisions.

clti.label: Figure 15
clti.caption: Clinical Decision-Making for Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia
clti.description: This flowchart outlines the decision-making process for managing patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI). It includes assessing limb threat severity, evaluating candidacy for limb salvage, and considering revascularization needs. The process involves staging the disease anatomically and determining the appropriate intervention based on risk assessment.

clti.label: Figure 16
clti.caption: Benefit of Revascularization in CLTI
clti.description: This chart illustrates the benefit of performing revascularization in patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) based on the severity of ischemia and limb severity. The benefit is categorized into high and low/nil, with color coding for easy interpretation.

clti.label: Figure 17
clti.caption: Revascularization Strategy Matrix for Infrapopliteal Disease
clti.description: This chart presents a matrix for determining the preferred initial revascularization strategy based on limb severity (WIfI stage) and anatomic complexity (GLASS stage). It categorizes treatment options into open bypass, endovascular, indeterminate, and no revascularization.

clti.label: Figure 18
clti.caption: Stairway to Amputation Risk Diagram
clti.description: This diagram illustrates the progressive stages leading to amputation in diabetes patients. It highlights the increasing risk from diabetes to neuropathy, ulceration, ischemia, infection, and finally amputation.

clti.label: Figure 19
clti.caption: Diabetic Foot Care Organization Flowchart
clti.description: This flowchart illustrates the hierarchical organization of diabetic foot care within a multidisciplinary team. It starts with Clinical Practice Guidelines and progresses through Clinical Practice Pathways, Policies and Procedures, Quality Assurance, and ends with Performance Improvement.

clti.label: Figure 20
clti.caption: Global Diabetes Projections by Region (2015-2040)
clti.description: This figure from the IDF Diabetes Atlas illustrates the projected increase in diabetes prevalence across different regions from 2015 to 2040. It highlights the significant rise in diabetes cases worldwide, with specific data for regions such as North America, Europe, and the Western Pacific.

clti.label: Table 4
clti.caption: List of Contributing Authors and Affiliations
clti.description: This table lists the contributing authors of the guideline, their affiliations, the sections they contributed to, and any conflicts of interest disclosures. It includes editors and steering committee members from various international institutions.

clti.label: Table 19
clti.caption: Classification Schemes for Chronic Limb Ischemia and Ulceration
clti.description: This table compares various classification systems used for assessing chronic limb ischemia and ulceration. It includes details on ischemic rest pain, ulcer, gangrene, ischemia, infection, and key features or comments for each system.

clti.label: Table 20
clti.caption: Classification Systems for Diabetic Foot Ulcers
clti.description: This table compares various classification systems for diabetic foot ulcers, focusing on ischemic rest pain, ulcer grading, gangrene, ischemia, infection, and key features. It includes systems from the University of Texas, SAD, PEDIS, Saint Elian, and IDSA.

clti.label: Table 21
clti.caption: SVS WIfI Threatened Limb Classification Overview (2014)
clti.description: This table presents the SVS WIfI classification system for chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI), detailing criteria for ischemic rest pain, ulcers, gangrene, ischemia, and infection. It highlights the grading system used to assess the severity and need for revascularization.

clti.label: Table 22
clti.caption: One-year Major Limb Amputation Rate by WiFI Stage
clti.description: This table presents the one-year major limb amputation rates categorized by the Society for Vascular Surgery's Wound, Ischemia, and foot Infection (WiFI) clinical stages. It includes data from multiple studies, showing the number of limbs at risk and the percentage of amputations at each stage.

clti.label: Table 26
clti.caption: Comparison of Noninvasive Testing Methods for CLTI
clti.description: This table compares various noninvasive testing methods used in patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI), highlighting their advantages and limitations. Techniques include AP/ABI, TP/TBI, segmental pressures, TcPO2, and skin perfusion pressure.

clti.label: Table 27
clti.caption: Ischemia Grading in WIfI Classification
clti.description: This table outlines the grading of ischemia in the Wound, Ischemia, and foot Infection (WIfI) classification. It categorizes ischemia into four grades based on ABI, ankle systolic pressure, and TP/TcPO2 measurements.

clti.label: Table 28
clti.caption: Foot Infection Grading in WIfI Classification
clti.description: This table outlines the grading system for foot infections within the Wound, Ischemia, and foot Infection (WIfI) classification. It categorizes infections based on clinical manifestations, assigning grades from 0 to 3, and correlates them with IDSA/PEDIS infection severity levels.

clti.label: Table 29
clti.caption: Clinical Stages of Limb Amputation Risk by WIfI Classification
clti.description: This table categorizes the risk of major limb amputation based on the Wound, Ischemia, and foot Infection (WIfI) classification. It outlines four stages of clinical risk from very low to high, with corresponding WIfI spectrum scores.

clti.label: Table 31
clti.caption: Comparison of Imaging Modalities for CLTI Patients
clti.description: This table compares different imaging techniques for patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI), highlighting their advantages and limitations. Techniques include DUS, CTA, MRA, and DSA, each with unique benefits and drawbacks in clinical application.

clti.label: Table 32
clti.caption: Research Priorities for CLTI Diagnosis and Limb Staging
clti.description: This table outlines research priorities focused on improving diagnostic and staging methods for Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia (CLTI). It includes recommendations for enhancing foot perfusion measurement, validating ultrasound techniques, reducing nephropathy risk, and improving imaging methods.

clti.label: Table 40
clti.caption: Research Priorities for Medical Management in CLTI
clti.description: This table outlines key research priorities for the medical management of patients with Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia (CLTI). It includes recommendations for optimizing antithrombotic regimens, lipid-lowering treatments, identifying predictive biomarkers, effective smoking cessation strategies, and analgesia for chronic pain.

clti.label: Table 42
clti.caption: Femoropopliteal Disease Grading in GLASS
clti.description: This table outlines the grading of femoropopliteal (FP) disease according to the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS). It categorizes the severity of disease from Grade 0 to Grade 4 based on the extent of superficial femoral artery (SFA) disease and popliteal artery involvement.

clti.label: Table 43
clti.caption: Infrapopliteal Disease Grading in GLASS System
clti.description: This table outlines the grading of infrapopliteal disease according to the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS). It categorizes the severity of arterial stenosis and occlusion in the tibial arteries, providing visual illustrations for each grade.

clti.label: Table 44
clti.caption: GLASS Stage Assignment Table
clti.description: This table assigns the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) stages based on femoropopliteal (FP) and infrapopliteal (IP) grades. It helps determine the severity of limb ischemia using angiographic images.

clti.label: Table 45
clti.caption: GLASS Stages of Infrainguinal Arterial Disease
clti.description: This table summarizes the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) for infrainguinal arterial disease, detailing stages based on estimated peripheral vascular intervention (PVI) outcomes and anatomic patterns. It provides a framework for assessing technical failure rates and limb-based patency (LBP) over one year.

clti.label: Table 46
clti.caption: Research Priorities for GLASS in Infrapopliteal Disease
clti.description: This table outlines research priorities for the Global Limb Anatomic Staging System (GLASS) focusing on infrapopliteal disease. It highlights key questions regarding procedural outcomes, disease grading, predictive measures, and treatment strategies.

clti.label: Table 53
clti.caption: Comparison of Risk Stratification Tools for CLTI Population
clti.description: This table compares various risk stratification tools used for assessing chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) patients. It lists different tools, their endpoints, critical factors considered, and references for each tool.

clti.label: Table 65
clti.caption: Research Priorities for Non-Revascularization Treatments of the Limb
clti.description: This table outlines research priorities focused on non-revascularization treatments for patients with Chronic Limb Threatening Ischemia (CLTI). It includes recommendations for assessing the effectiveness of various therapies such as pneumatic compression, exercise therapy, and hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT).

clti.label: Table 66
clti.caption: Major Trials of Gene and Cell Therapy in Vascular Conditions
clti.description: This table summarizes major clinical trials comparing gene and cell therapies for vascular conditions, focusing on endpoints like amputation-free survival (AFS) and other clinical outcomes. It includes details on the number of participants, treatments, and significant findings.

clti.label: Table 68
clti.caption: Outcomes of Major Lower Extremity Amputation
clti.description: This table presents the outcomes associated with different levels of major lower extremity amputation: below knee, through knee, and above knee. It includes data on primary healing rates, perioperative mortality, revision to a higher level, and ambulation success.

clti.label: Table 71
clti.caption: Research Priorities for Amputation Techniques
clti.description: This table outlines research priorities focused on determining optimal amputation techniques and rehabilitation strategies. It emphasizes the need for noninvasive testing, comparison of healing rates, and quality of life assessments post-amputation.

clti.label: Table 79
clti.caption: IDEAL Stages for CLTI Innovation
clti.description: This table outlines the stages of surgical and endovascular innovation for chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) using the IDEAL framework. It details the progression from idea to long-term study, highlighting patient selection, specialist involvement, procedural development, and outcome measures.

clti.label: Table 82
clti.caption: Comparison of End Points in BASIL and BEST-CLI Trials
clti.description: This table compares the primary and secondary end points of the BASIL-2, BASIL-3, and BEST-CLI trials, which evaluate different interventions for severe leg ischemia. It highlights various clinical outcomes such as survival, morbidity, and quality of life.

clti.label: Table 85
clti.caption: Research Priorities for CLTI Study Design and Endpoints
clti.description: This table outlines key research priorities for designing studies and determining trial endpoints in Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia (CLTI). It includes recommendations for constructing relevant RCTs, clarifying revascularization methods, and validating assessment tools.

clti.label: Table 86
clti.caption: Three Tiers of Care for Amputation Prevention and Diabetic Foot Care Centers
clti.description: This table outlines three levels of care for preventing amputations and managing diabetic foot care. It details the settings, potential clinicians involved, and their roles at each level: basic, intermediate, and center of excellence.

clti.label: Table 87
clti.caption: Criteria for Center of Excellence in CLTI or Amputation Prevention
clti.description: This table outlines the criteria for a Center of Excellence designation in managing chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) or preventing amputation. It includes the need for a multidisciplinary team, protocol-driven care, outcomes monitoring, improvement methods, and educational resources.

clti.label: Table 88
clti.caption: Essential Skills for Preventing Amputations in Diabetes
clti.description: This table outlines nine essential skills necessary to prevent amputations in diabetic patients, along with the corresponding medical specialties responsible for each skill. It emphasizes a multidisciplinary approach involving various healthcare professionals.

clti.label: Table 89
clti.caption: Outcome Measures for CLTI and Amputation Prevention
clti.description: This table outlines major outcome measures for chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI) and strategies to prevent amputation. It includes calculations for limb salvage rates, amputation ratios, healing percentages, and revascularization success.

clti.label: Table 91
clti.caption: Estimated PAD Prevalence Change by Age and Region (2000-2010)
clti.description: This table presents the estimated rate of change in the number of people living with peripheral artery disease (PAD) from 2000 to 2010, segmented by age groups and regions (High-income countries vs. Low- and middle-income countries).

clti.label: Table 92
clti.caption: Prevalence of PAD in Diabetics by Country and Year
clti.description: This table presents the prevalence percentages of peripheral artery disease (PAD) among diabetics across various African countries over three time periods: 1990-2000, 2000-2010, and 2010. Data availability varies by country and period.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 5
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Ultrasound Measurement of Aortic Diameter
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This image illustrates the placement of calipers for measuring the aortic diameter using ultrasound. It shows three measurement methods: inner to inner (ITI), leading edge to leading edge (LELE), and outer to outer (OTO).

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 20
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: EVAR Sizing and Planning Worksheet Diagram
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This diagram is a worksheet for planning endovascular abdominal aortic repair (EVAR). It includes sections for patient information, anatomical measurements, and stent graft details. The diagram provides a visual guide for measuring and recording key dimensions and angles necessary for EVAR device planning.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 30
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Management Algorithm for Abdominal Compartment Syndrome in AAA Repair
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This flowchart outlines the management of abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS) in patients undergoing open or endovascular repair of ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysms. It details the steps based on intra-abdominal pressure (IAP) levels and the presence of organ failure.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 32
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Flowchart for Diagnosing Colonic Ischaemia Post-AAA Repair
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This flowchart outlines the diagnostic process for colonic ischaemia in patients after ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm repair. It categorizes suspicion levels into no suspicion, moderate suspicion, and high suspicion, guiding subsequent actions such as watchful waiting, sigmoidoscopy, or immediate laparotomy.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 34
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Benign Mural Thrombus in Stent Graft Imaging
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This image shows a computed tomography angiography of a stent graft with a benign mural thrombus formation. The thrombus is located inside the main body of the stent graft, attributed to a barrel shape configuration.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 39
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Follow-up Algorithm After Standard EVAR
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This flowchart outlines the recommended follow-up procedure after standard endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR). It guides clinicians through decision points based on post-operative CTA findings, presence of endoleaks, and sac dynamics to determine the need for re-intervention or continued monitoring.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 40
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Diagnostic Steps for Occult Endoleak Detection
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This flowchart outlines a step-by-step diagnostic approach for detecting occult endoleaks following endovascular abdominal aortic aneurysm repair. It progresses from initial imaging techniques to more invasive procedures if necessary.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 41
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Anatomical Classification of Complex AAAs
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This diagram illustrates the anatomical classification of complex abdominal aortic aneurysms (AAAs) based on their proximal extensions relative to the renal arteries (RAs), superior mesenteric artery (SMA), and coeliac artery (CA). It categorizes AAAs into five types: short neck infrarenal, juxtarenal, pararenal, paravisceral, and type IV thoraco-abdominal aortic aneurysm.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 42
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Illustration of EVAR Configurations
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This figure illustrates three different configurations of endovascular aortic repair (EVAR): fenestrated EVAR (fEVAR), branched EVAR (bEVAR), and a combination of both (f/bEVAR). Each configuration is depicted to show how they accommodate different anatomical variations of the aorta.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 43
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Endoleaks in Failed Bridging Stents for Aortic Repair
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This diagram illustrates various types of endoleaks associated with failed bridging stents in fenestrated and branched endovascular aortic repair. It categorizes the endoleaks based on their location and cause, such as proximal attachment, retrograde flow, and graft porosity.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 46
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Isolated Iliac Artery Aneurysm Classification Diagram
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This diagram illustrates the classification of isolated iliac artery aneurysms into four types (I-IV) based on their anatomical presentation. Each type shows a different configuration of the aneurysm in relation to the iliac artery branches.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 53
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Illustration of Aortic Pathologies and Aneurysms
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This diagram illustrates various aortic pathologies including aortic dissection, intramural hematoma, penetrating aortic ulcer, pseudoaneurysm, saccular aneurysm, and fusiform aneurysm. Each condition is depicted to show differences in the aortic wall structure and potential complications.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 54
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Shared Decision Making in Patient Care
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This diagram illustrates the concept of shared decision making in patient care, highlighting the integration of evidence-based medicine and patient preferences. It emphasizes the importance of balancing clinical evidence with individual patient values to achieve optimal healthcare outcomes.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Figure 56
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Repair Methods
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This diagram illustrates three stages of addressing an abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA). Part A shows the location of a simple AAA. Part B depicts open surgery where the affected aorta segment is replaced with a graft. Part C shows endovascular repair using a stent graft to reinforce the aorta.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 4
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: ESC Evidence Grading System for Recommendations
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table outlines the European Society of Cardiology (ESC) evidence grading system for clinical recommendations. It categorizes recommendations into classes based on the level of evidence and consensus regarding the efficacy of treatments or procedures.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 5
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Levels of Evidence in ESC Grading System
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table outlines the levels of evidence as adapted from the European Society of Cardiology (ESC) grading system. It categorizes evidence into three levels based on the source and type of data, ranging from multiple randomized trials to expert opinions.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 13
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Summary of Randomised Trials for AAA Screening in Men
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table summarizes four randomized trials on population-based screening for abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) in men. It includes details such as the number of participants, age range, recruitment period, and outcomes like AAA detection and mortality rates.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 16
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Screening Potential for Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm by Risk Group
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table evaluates the suitability for abdominal aortic aneurysm screening across different risk groups, distinguishing between men and women. It uses a grading system to indicate the level of screening potential.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 20
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: RCTs on Medications for Slowing Aneurysm Growth
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table summarizes randomized controlled trials evaluating various medications aimed at slowing the growth of small aneurysms. It includes details on the study name, treatment evaluated, number of patients included, and the main findings regarding aneurysm growth rates.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 24
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Studies on Rupture Risk of Small Abdominal Aortic Aneurysms
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table summarizes various studies on the rupture risk of small abdominal aortic aneurysms (AAAs), detailing recruitment periods, measurement modalities, and rupture rates. It highlights the threshold diameters used in each study and the corresponding rupture rates observed.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 30
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Risk Factors for Complications Post-AAA Repair
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table outlines the risk factors for cardiac, respiratory, and renal complications following abdominal aortic aneurysm repair. It categorizes predictors into three groups: cardiac, pulmonary, and renal complications, providing specific clinical conditions and measurements associated with increased risk.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 31
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Functional Capacity Estimation Based on Physical Activity Levels
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table categorizes functional capacity based on physical activity levels, measured in metabolic equivalents (METs). It provides examples of activities corresponding to different MET ranges, from poor to excellent capacity.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 52
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Anatomical Requirements for Stent Grafts Usage
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table outlines the anatomical requirements for the use of stent grafts in vascular procedures. It includes measurements such as diameters, lengths, and angles necessary for proper stent graft application.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 57
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Comparison of EVAR and Open Repair for Abdominal Aortic Aneurysms
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table summarizes randomized controlled trials comparing elective endovascular aortic repair (EVAR) and open surgical repair for abdominal aortic aneurysms. It includes studies from the UK, Netherlands, Belgium, USA, and France, highlighting peri-operative mortality, survival benefits, and re-intervention rates.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 58
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Comparison of EVAR and Open Repair for Abdominal Aortic Aneurysms: Meta-Analysis Summary
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table summarizes findings from various studies comparing elective endovascular aortic repair (EVAR) and open surgical repair for abdominal aortic aneurysms. It includes study types, recruitment periods, patient numbers, and main findings regarding mortality and re-intervention rates.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 69
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Comparison of Peri-operative Mortality in EVAR vs OSR
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table compares peri-operative mortality rates between endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) and open surgical repair (OSR) for patients with ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm. It includes data from various studies published from 2010 onwards, with more than 1,000 patients each.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 70
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Peri-operative Mortality in RCTs for Ruptured AAA Repair
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table presents peri-operative mortality rates from four randomized controlled trials comparing endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) and open surgical repair (OSR) for ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA). It includes data on recruitment periods, patient numbers, and 30-day mortality rates for both EVAR and OSR.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 72
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Medical Treatment Options for Intra-abdominal Hypertension and Abdominal Compartment Syndrome
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table outlines various medical treatment strategies for managing intra-abdominal hypertension and abdominal compartment syndrome. It categorizes interventions into improving abdominal wall compliance, evacuating abdominal contents, correcting fluid balance, and supporting organ function.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 79
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Incidence of Complications Post-AAA Repair
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table outlines the estimated incidence of various complications following abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) repair, within five years and between 10 to 15 years post-surgery.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 80
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Long-term Complications After EVAR of AAA
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table outlines the long-term complications following endovascular repair of abdominal aortic aneurysm (EVAR). It categorizes complications by type, provides their meanings, estimates their incidence within five years, and assesses rupture risk.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 103
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Comparison of Imaging Modalities for EVAR Follow-up
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table compares the pros and cons of different imaging modalities used for follow-up after endovascular abdominal aortic aneurysm repair (EVAR). It evaluates availability, cost, complication detection, aortic branch assessment, operator dependency, nephrotoxicity, and radiation exposure across CTA, CT, DUS, CEUS, and MRA.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 126
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Diagnostic Criteria for Mycotic Abdominal Aortic Aneurysms
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table outlines the suggested diagnostic criteria for identifying mycotic abdominal aortic aneurysms. It includes clinical presentations, laboratory tests, radiological findings, and surgical presentations that are indicative of this condition.

abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.label: Table 141
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.caption: Comparison of AAA Surgery Types: Endovascular vs. Open Repair
abdominal_aortic_aneurysm.description: This table compares the advantages and disadvantages of two types of abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) surgeries: endovascular repair and open surgical repair. It highlights factors such as recovery time, risk of death, and need for further operations.
